texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas-solvent contactors are a hybrid approach that has been applied to carbon capture and storage (CCS) [1,2]. The contactor consists of a permeable membrane separating the solvent and gas phases, which overcomes many of the limitations of existing solvent absorption and membrane technologies. The use of a solvent ensures high selectivity for CO2, while the membrane provides a high mass transfer area and the membrane module enables the flow regimes of the two phases to be separately controlled. The majority of the research on membrane contactors has focused on porous membranes for the absorption of CO2 into solvents, such as Monoethanolamine (MEA) [3-5]. Hence, membrane contactors have the possibility to replace traditional solvent absorber columns and correspondingly reduce the footprint of the carbon capture process [6]. The application of porous membrane contactors however, has suffered from the effects of pore wetting by the solvent and reduced mass transfer performance. Recent research has focused on thin film composite membrane contactors for the absorption of CO2 [7-10], because the non-porous layer is believed to prevent pore wetting. Highly permeable polymeric
texts.text: Membrane gas-solvent contactors are a hybrid approach that has been applied to carbon capture and storage (CCS) [1,2]. The contactor consists of a permeable membrane separating the solvent and gas phases, which overcomes many of the limitations of existing solvent absorption and membrane technologies. The use of a solvent ensures high selectivity for CO2, while the membrane provides a high mass transfer area and the membrane module enables the flow regimes of the two phases to be separately controlled. The majority of the research on membrane contactors has focused on porous membranes for the absorption of CO2 into solvents, such as Monoethanolamine (MEA) [3-5]. Hence, membrane contactors have the possibility to replace traditional solvent absorber columns and correspondingly reduce the footprint of the carbon capture process [6]. The application of porous membrane contactors however, has suffered from the effects of pore wetting by the solvent and reduced mass transfer performance. Recent research has focused on thin film composite membrane contactors for the absorption of CO2 [7-10], because the non-porous layer is believed to prevent pore wetting. Highly permeable polymeric

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas solvent contactors are a hybrid approach that shows potential to be more efficient for carbon dioxide capture than traditional packed columns. Here, three non-porous composite membrane contactors are trialed for desorption of CO2 from loaded Monoethanolamine (MEA) at temperatures 70 ° C and above. These are non-porous poly (1-trimethylsilyl-1-propyne) (PTMSP), Polymer of Intrinsic Microporosity (PIM-1) and Teflon AF1600, all on a porous PP support. The CO2 regeneration flux was shown to increase with temperature because of increasing driving force across the membrane. Similarly, the CO2 flux increased with solvent Reynolds number because of increasing turbulence in the solvent boundary layer. The overall mass transfer coefficient for the three membrane contactors were calculated and demonstrated that desorption was a mass transfer limiting process, with over 90% of the resistance corresponding to the solvent boundary layer. The water vapor fluxes through the non-porous membrane contactors were also measured and highlighted that water permeation was greater than CO2 for all three membrane contactor systems.
texts.text: Membrane gas solvent contactors are a hybrid approach that shows potential to be more efficient for carbon dioxide capture than traditional packed columns. Here, three non-porous composite membrane contactors are trialed for desorption of CO2 from loaded Monoethanolamine (MEA) at temperatures 70 ° C and above. These are non-porous poly (1-trimethylsilyl-1-propyne) (PTMSP), Polymer of Intrinsic Microporosity (PIM-1) and Teflon AF1600, all on a porous PP support. The CO2 regeneration flux was shown to increase with temperature because of increasing driving force across the membrane. Similarly, the CO2 flux increased with solvent Reynolds number because of increasing turbulence in the solvent boundary layer. The overall mass transfer coefficient for the three membrane contactors were calculated and demonstrated that desorption was a mass transfer limiting process, with over 90% of the resistance corresponding to the solvent boundary layer. The water vapor fluxes through the non-porous membrane contactors were also measured and highlighted that water permeation was greater than CO2 for all three membrane contactor systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: membranes are required to ensure the mass transfer resistance of this non-porous layer is minimal. However, this approach has not always been successful at preventing pore wetting of the porous support layer, because of high water flux through the nonporous layer [8,11]. The regeneration of CO2 loaded solvent, also known as stripping, through membrane contactors is an area that has been less studied, even though it is complementary to solvent absorption. Early work by Kosaraju et al. [11] studied CO2 stripping in both a porous polypropylene contactor with a non-volatile solvent as well as an ultra-thin dense asymmetric poly (4-methyl-1pentene) membrane with MEA. They found that separation performance was strongly impacted by solvent volatility and its ability to wet the porous support layer. Simioni et al. [12] studied porous poly tetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and a polyether sulfone membrane with a hydrophobic surface coating for the stripping of CO2 from potassium carbonate. They found that minimizing pore wetting through increasing the hydrophobicity of the membrane pores was necessary to ensure high mass transfer rates. Similar performance was observed by Khaisri et al. [13,14] for porous PTFE membrane stripping CO2 from loaded MEA. Wang et al. [15] studied a range of amine-based solvents for the regeneration of CO2 through a vacuum regeneration process using a microporous polypropylene (PP) contactor. Importantly, they found that the regeneration through a membrane contactor was competitive with traditional

Early work by Kosaraju et al. They found that separation performance was strongly impacted by solvent volatility and its ability to wet the porous support layer. Simioni et al. [12] studied porous poly tetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and a polyether sulfone membrane with a hydrophobic surface coating for the stripping of CO2 from potassium carbonate. They found that minimizing pore wetting through increasing the hydrophobicity of the membrane pores was necessary to ensure high mass transfer rates. Similar performance was observed by Khaisri et al. [13,14] for porous PTFE membrane stripping CO2 from loaded MEA. Wang et al. texts.label: text
texts.orig: solvent regeneration approaches. Porous ceramic membranes have also been applied to CO2 stripping from MEA [16]. The study of thin film composite membrane contactors for CO2 regeneration has been limited to the early study by Kosaraju et al. [11] with an asymmetric PMP membrane and Dibrov et al. [17], who studied a composite membrane consisting of non-porous poly(1trimethylsilyl-1-propyne) (PTMSP) layer with MDEA solution. That study obtained high CO2 regeneration fluxes through the membrane, but the age dependence of PTMSP morphology meant that the flux of CO2 decreased substantially with time.
texts.text: solvent regeneration approaches. Porous ceramic membranes have also been applied to CO2 stripping from MEA [16]. The study of thin film composite membrane contactors for CO2 regeneration has been limited to the early study by Kosaraju et al. [11] with an asymmetric PMP membrane and Dibrov et al. [17], who studied a composite membrane consisting of non-porous poly(1trimethylsilyl-1-propyne) (PTMSP) layer with MDEA solution. That study obtained high CO2 regeneration fluxes through the membrane, but the age dependence of PTMSP morphology meant that the flux of CO2 decreased substantially with time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here, three hollow-fiber thin-film composite membrane contactors are studied for the stripping of CO2 from loaded MEA solution at elevated temperatures from 70 to 105 ° C. The three contactors consist of non-porous PTMSP, a non-porous Polymer of Intrinsic Microporosity (PIM-1) and non-porous perfluorinated polymer Teflon AF1600 on a porous polypropylene (PP) support, all of which have previously been investigated for solvent absorption [8]. The purpose of this investigation was to study the CO2 flux and mass transfer through the membrane contactors as a function of solvent flowrate and temperature. Furthermore, the rate limiting process is determined from mass transfer theory to provide insight into how the contactors operated under solvent stripping conditions. PTMSP and Polymer of Intrinsic Microporosity (PIM-1) were chosen because of their high CO2 permeances [18,19] and Teflon AF1600 was chosen because of its unique solvent properties [20,21]. In addition, the water flux through the three composite membrane contactors as a function of temperature was investigated.
texts.text: Here, three hollow-fiber thin-film composite membrane contactors are studied for the stripping of CO2 from loaded MEA solution at elevated temperatures from 70 to 105 ° C. The three contactors consist of non-porous PTMSP, a non-porous Polymer of Intrinsic Microporosity (PIM-1) and non-porous perfluorinated polymer Teflon AF1600 on a porous polypropylene (PP) support, all of which have previously been investigated for solvent absorption [8]. The purpose of this investigation was to study the CO2 flux and mass transfer through the membrane contactors as a function of solvent flowrate and temperature. Furthermore, the rate limiting process is determined from mass transfer theory to provide insight into how the contactors operated under solvent stripping conditions. PTMSP and Polymer of Intrinsic Microporosity (PIM-1) were chosen because of their high CO2 permeances [18,19] and Teflon AF1600 was chosen because of its unique solvent properties [20,21]. In addition, the water flux through the three composite membrane contactors as a function of temperature was investigated.

texts.orig: 2. Theory
texts.text: 2. Theory

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall mass transfer across the membrane contactor consists of three mass transfer stages, the transfer of CO2 across the solvent boundary layer, the transfer of CO2 through the membrane and finally the transfer of CO2 across the gas boundary layer. These stages act as a resistance to mass transfer and are usually expressed as a series [2,13]:
texts.text: The overall mass transfer across the membrane contactor consists of three mass transfer stages, the transfer of CO2 across the solvent boundary layer, the transfer of CO2 through the membrane and finally the transfer of CO2 across the gas boundary layer. These stages act as a resistance to mass transfer and are usually expressed as a series [2,13]:

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Experimental
texts.text: 3. Experimental

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thin film composite membranes consisting of a non-porous dense outer layer of Teflon AF1600 (DuPont, USA), PTMSP (Fluorochem Ltd, UK) and PIM-1 (the polycondensation product of ultrahigh purity monomers of 5,5 0 ,6,6 0 -tetrahydroxy-3,3,3 0 ,3 0 -tetramethyl-1,1 0 -spirobisindane (TTSBI) and 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroter ephthalonitrile (TFTPN)) on porous polypropylene fibers (Membrana, USA) were produced through standard dip coating techniques, with the details reported elsewhere [8]. All fibers were vacuum annealed at 50 ° C overnight to evaporate any residual solvent in the active layer. Membrane contactor modules were potted from 10 fibers of each membrane in a stainless steel ¼ 00 tube approximately 30 cm long, sealed with epoxy resin. To avoid aging effects in the PTMSP and PIM-1 active layers, methanol was added to the shell side of the contactor for a 1 h period before the experiment [18,27], then dried with air before the experiment began. The Teflon AF1600 membrane had no pretreatment undertaken. Specifics of the composite membranes are provided in Table 1. For each temperature range a new contactor module was used to ensure hydrodynamics within the non-porous and porous layers was the same. All contactor measurements were run for 2 h, to
texts.text: Thin film composite membranes consisting of a non-porous dense outer layer of Teflon AF1600 (DuPont, USA), PTMSP (Fluorochem Ltd, UK) and PIM-1 (the polycondensation product of ultrahigh purity monomers of 5,5 0 ,6,6 0 -tetrahydroxy-3,3,3 0 ,3 0 -tetramethyl-1,1 0 -spirobisindane (TTSBI) and 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroter ephthalonitrile (TFTPN)) on porous polypropylene fibers (Membrana, USA) were produced through standard dip coating techniques, with the details reported elsewhere [8]. All fibers were vacuum annealed at 50 ° C overnight to evaporate any residual solvent in the active layer. Membrane contactor modules were potted from 10 fibers of each membrane in a stainless steel ¼ 00 tube approximately 30 cm long, sealed with epoxy resin. To avoid aging effects in the PTMSP and PIM-1 active layers, methanol was added to the shell side of the contactor for a 1 h period before the experiment [18,27], then dried with air before the experiment began. The Teflon AF1600 membrane had no pretreatment undertaken. Specifics of the composite membranes are provided in Table 1. For each temperature range a new contactor module was used to ensure hydrodynamics within the non-porous and porous layers was the same. All contactor measurements were run for 2 h, to

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Monoethanolamine (MEA) was supplied by Merck (Canada) and made-up to a 30 wt% solution. CO2 was bubbled through the solvent to obtain an average loading of 0.49 for the desorption measurements. Details of the solvent properties with temperature assumed in this research are provided in Table 2, which take into account the high CO2 loading and taken from the literature [28,29]. Five temperatures were studied, 70, 80, 90, 100 and 105 ° C, the last corresponding to the boiling temperature of MEA solution. The membrane contactor solvent regeneration apparatus is based on a modified version of that already reported in the literature [13] (Fig. 1). The solvent was pumped (Cole-Parmer model K75211-60) from a 50 L storage tank through an oil bath heater (Cole-Parmer) set at the desired temperature. The heated solvent then entered the membrane contactor on the shell side in a counter current flow regime to the sweep gas.
texts.text: Monoethanolamine (MEA) was supplied by Merck (Canada) and made-up to a 30 wt% solution. CO2 was bubbled through the solvent to obtain an average loading of 0.49 for the desorption measurements. Details of the solvent properties with temperature assumed in this research are provided in Table 2, which take into account the high CO2 loading and taken from the literature [28,29]. Five temperatures were studied, 70, 80, 90, 100 and 105 ° C, the last corresponding to the boiling temperature of MEA solution. The membrane contactor solvent regeneration apparatus is based on a modified version of that already reported in the literature [13] (Fig. 1). The solvent was pumped (Cole-Parmer model K75211-60) from a 50 L storage tank through an oil bath heater (Cole-Parmer) set at the desired temperature. The heated solvent then entered the membrane contactor on the shell side in a counter current flow regime to the sweep gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To ensure high flux through the membrane and prevent concentration polarization on the gas side, a N2 sweep gas is applied to the lumen side at  120 kPa and with a flowrate of 1 L/min (STP), controlled through a mass flow controller (Aalborg). The regenerated solvent exited the membrane contactor and was cooled through a plate and frame cross heat exchanger. The cooled solvent was then passed through a rotameter (Cole-Parmer EW-03219-27), before entering the lean solvent storage tank. The membrane contactor and hot solvent lines were thermally insulated, and thermocouples placed throughout the apparatus enabled the temperature to be monitored and controlled through the oil bath setting. The N2 sweep gas flowrate was pre-heated through the heat exchanger to ensure constant temperature within the contactor. The exit sweep gas was cooled through a condenser (water-feed) to  5 ° C and passed to a separator. The amount of condensed water in this separator was measured by volume during steady state operation to provide the water flux through the membrane contactor. The exit sweep gas was then passed through a dedicated CO2 IR analyzer (Nova Analytical Systems, model 302). The feed and regenerated solvent CO2 loading were both analyzed through standard methodologies based on Chittick CO2 Analyzer titration [30]. The CO2 permeability of flat sheet samples of the three thin film composite layers, previously synthesized [31,32], was measured separately on a constant volume variable pressure apparatus previously described in the literature [33].
texts.text: To ensure high flux through the membrane and prevent concentration polarization on the gas side, a N2 sweep gas is applied to the lumen side at  120 kPa and with a flowrate of 1 L/min (STP), controlled through a mass flow controller (Aalborg). The regenerated solvent exited the membrane contactor and was cooled through a plate and frame cross heat exchanger. The cooled solvent was then passed through a rotameter (Cole-Parmer EW-03219-27), before entering the lean solvent storage tank. The membrane contactor and hot solvent lines were thermally insulated, and thermocouples placed throughout the apparatus enabled the temperature to be monitored and controlled through the oil bath setting. The N2 sweep gas flowrate was pre-heated through the heat exchanger to ensure constant temperature within the contactor. The exit sweep gas was cooled through a condenser (water-feed) to  5 ° C and passed to a separator. The amount of condensed water in this separator was measured by volume during steady state operation to provide the water flux through the membrane contactor. The exit sweep gas was then passed through a dedicated CO2 IR analyzer (Nova Analytical Systems, model 302). The feed and regenerated solvent CO2 loading were both analyzed through standard methodologies based on Chittick CO2 Analyzer titration [30]. The CO2 permeability of flat sheet samples of the three thin film composite layers, previously synthesized [31,32], was measured separately on a constant volume variable pressure apparatus previously described in the literature [33].

texts.text: 4.1. Gas permeability

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 permeability for all three of the polymers used increases with temperature (Fig. 2), which is standard behavior and associated with increasing gas diffusivity [33]. PTMSP has the highest CO2 permeability, associated with the very high fractional free volume of the polymer [19]. The permeability of PIM1 is  20% of the PTMSP permeability over the temperature range studied. The Teflon AF1600 CO2 permeabilities are an order of

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Overall mass transfer coefficients
texts.text: 4.2. Overall mass transfer coefficients

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 fluxes observed through the three membrane contactors are consistent with the prior literature. For example, Dibrov et al. [17] observe a flux of 0.2-1.6 m 3 /(m 2 h) (0.3-2 mol/(m 2 s)) for a thin film composite PTMSP contactor with MDEA at 100 ° C, which is comparable to our own value of 1-3 mol/(m 2 s) with MEA. Similarly, all three non-porous contactors provide a comparable CO2 flux at 70 ° C to that reported by Fang et al. [34] for a porous PP contactor when the solvent was shell side.
texts.text: The CO2 fluxes observed through the three membrane contactors are consistent with the prior literature. For example, Dibrov et al. [17] observe a flux of 0.2-1.6 m 3 /(m 2 h) (0.3-2 mol/(m 2 s)) for a thin film composite PTMSP contactor with MDEA at 100 ° C, which is comparable to our own value of 1-3 mol/(m 2 s) with MEA. Similarly, all three non-porous contactors provide a comparable CO2 flux at 70 ° C to that reported by Fang et al. [34] for a porous PP contactor when the solvent was shell side.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These fluxes can be used to determine the overall mass transfer coefficient through the three membrane contactors as a function of solvent Reynolds number and temperature (Fig. 3). As expected, the mass transfer coefficient increases with the solvent Reynolds number and also with the solvent temperature because the reduced solution viscosity increases mixing through faster diffusivity and faster reaction kinetics as temperature increases.
texts.text: These fluxes can be used to determine the overall mass transfer coefficient through the three membrane contactors as a function of solvent Reynolds number and temperature (Fig. 3). As expected, the mass transfer coefficient increases with the solvent Reynolds number and also with the solvent temperature because the reduced solution viscosity increases mixing through faster diffusivity and faster reaction kinetics as temperature increases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There is little difference in mass transfer coefficient between the PTMSP and PIM-1 contactor over the temperature range studied. Conversely, the mass transfer coefficients for the Teflon AF1600 contactor are lower by up to 15% than those observed for PTMSP and PIM-1, which is most evident at 100 and 105 ° C. This is attributed to the thicker non-porous layer (12 versus 2 l m) and the lower permeability of this polymer.
texts.text: There is little difference in mass transfer coefficient between the PTMSP and PIM-1 contactor over the temperature range studied. Conversely, the mass transfer coefficients for the Teflon AF1600 contactor are lower by up to 15% than those observed for PTMSP and PIM-1, which is most evident at 100 and 105 ° C. This is attributed to the thicker non-porous layer (12 versus 2 l m) and the lower permeability of this polymer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The mass transfer coefficients obtained here are an order of magnitude greater than those observed by Kosaraju et al. [11] for a PMP contactor with MEA, which is most likely due to their use of a helium sweep gas and ambient temperature for regeneration, and therefore there is no enhancement for the regeneration. Hence, they did not have the benefit of the higher temperatures of this study. The membrane contactors have comparable overall mass transfer coefficients to that reported by Khaisri et al. [13] and Fang et al. [34] for porous PP contactors with MEA, as well as that reported by Simioni et al. [12] for porous contactors with potassium carbonate, undertaken over the same temperature range.
texts.text: The mass transfer coefficients obtained here are an order of magnitude greater than those observed by Kosaraju et al. [11] for a PMP contactor with MEA, which is most likely due to their use of a helium sweep gas and ambient temperature for regeneration, and therefore there is no enhancement for the regeneration. Hence, they did not have the benefit of the higher temperatures of this study. The membrane contactors have comparable overall mass transfer coefficients to that reported by Khaisri et al. [13] and Fang et al. [34] for porous PP contactors with MEA, as well as that reported by Simioni et al. [12] for porous contactors with potassium carbonate, undertaken over the same temperature range.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The relationship between the Reynolds, Schmidt and Sherwood numbers (Eq. (4)) with temperature enables the rate limiting process for the membrane contactors to be determined. This approach assumes the mass transfer in the gas phase and porous membrane is negligible, while the rate of reaction change with temperature is extrapolated from literature [35]. The dimensionless numbers relationship is provided in Fig. 4 for all three contactors. It is clear that for 80, 90 and 100 ° C, the data set for the three contactors converge, indicative of a mass transfer limiting process due to the solvent boundary layer. The gradient of those data points is comparable to that expected from the Yang and Cussler solvent boundary layer model [24]. At 70 ° C, the relationship has a lower magnitude and lower gradient for the three contactors, indicative of limitation in the mass transfer process. At 105 ° C, the relationship has the same gradient as lower temperatures, but a greater magnitude. This deviation is associated with the solvent boiling, and hence altering the mass transfer resistance. This is further supported by the calculated ratio of CO2 mass transfer diffusion flux against reaction flux for loaded MEA across the temperature range studied, as shown in Fig. 5, where the calculated reaction flux is
texts.text: The relationship between the Reynolds, Schmidt and Sherwood numbers (Eq. (4)) with temperature enables the rate limiting process for the membrane contactors to be determined. This approach assumes the mass transfer in the gas phase and porous membrane is negligible, while the rate of reaction change with temperature is extrapolated from literature [35]. The dimensionless numbers relationship is provided in Fig. 4 for all three contactors. It is clear that for 80, 90 and 100 ° C, the data set for the three contactors converge, indicative of a mass transfer limiting process due to the solvent boundary layer. The gradient of those data points is comparable to that expected from the Yang and Cussler solvent boundary layer model [24]. At 70 ° C, the relationship has a lower magnitude and lower gradient for the three contactors, indicative of limitation in the mass transfer process. At 105 ° C, the relationship has the same gradient as lower temperatures, but a greater magnitude. This deviation is associated with the solvent boiling, and hence altering the mass transfer resistance. This is further supported by the calculated ratio of CO2 mass transfer diffusion flux against reaction flux for loaded MEA across the temperature range studied, as shown in Fig. 5, where the calculated reaction flux is

texts.label: text
texts.orig: extrapolate from Jamal et al. [23]. This normalized ratio clearly demonstrates that as temperatures increases the mass transfer diffusion flux decreases relative to the reaction flux, indicating that the process becomes increasingly mass transfer limited. At 80 ° C and above, the ratio becomes constant indicative that trade-off between mass transfer and reaction rate becomes steady-state, and supportive of the relationship observed in Fig. 4.
texts.text: extrapolate from Jamal et al. [23]. This normalized ratio clearly demonstrates that as temperatures increases the mass transfer diffusion flux decreases relative to the reaction flux, indicating that the process becomes increasingly mass transfer limited. At 80 ° C and above, the ratio becomes constant indicative that trade-off between mass transfer and reaction rate becomes steady-state, and supportive of the relationship observed in Fig. 4.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The contribution of the solvent boundary layer to the overall mass transfer coefficient resistance can be estimated based on the correlations for mass transfer given in Eqs. (2)-(4), with the Graetz number varying between 1.2 and 1.3. At 70 ° C the solvent boundary layer accounts for between 95% and 98% of the total mass transfer resistance for the three contactors, while increasing the temperature to 105 ° C, the solvent boundary layer resistance becomes 90% and 96% of the total mass transfer resistance, dependent on solvent Reynolds No. Hence, it is clear that the solvent boundary layer dominates mass transfer resistance. This result is comparable to that reported by Fang et al. [34] and Khaisri et al. [13] for porous PP contactors. There was no evidence of wetting within the porous support of the three contactors.
texts.text: The contribution of the solvent boundary layer to the overall mass transfer coefficient resistance can be estimated based on the correlations for mass transfer given in Eqs. (2)-(4), with the Graetz number varying between 1.2 and 1.3. At 70 ° C the solvent boundary layer accounts for between 95% and 98% of the total mass transfer resistance for the three contactors, while increasing the temperature to 105 ° C, the solvent boundary layer resistance becomes 90% and 96% of the total mass transfer resistance, dependent on solvent Reynolds No. Hence, it is clear that the solvent boundary layer dominates mass transfer resistance. This result is comparable to that reported by Fang et al. [34] and Khaisri et al. [13] for porous PP contactors. There was no evidence of wetting within the porous support of the three contactors.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Membrane contactor water permeability
texts.text: 4.3. Membrane contactor water permeability

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The averaged water permeability through the non-porous layer of the three contactors as a function of temperature is provided in Fig. 6, where the driving force is from the hot solvent into the dry sweep gas. The increase permeability with temperature is associated with increased diffusivity through the non-porous layer. For all three contactors, the water permeability is always greater than the CO2 permeability through the contactor and indicates that the contactor operating under these conditions will loss water into the CO2 product stream and concentrate the solvent. The water permeability for PTMSP is similar to literature [31] and indicates that competitive sorption of CO2 and MEA on water is not significant within the PTMSP membrane contactor. In contrast, the water permeability through PIM-1 and Teflon AF1600 is lower than literature [31,32]. This may be due to MEA and CO2 competitive sorption on water permeability or alternatively pore wetting changing the hydrodynamics of the porous support.
texts.text: The averaged water permeability through the non-porous layer of the three contactors as a function of temperature is provided in Fig. 6, where the driving force is from the hot solvent into the dry sweep gas. The increase permeability with temperature is associated with increased diffusivity through the non-porous layer. For all three contactors, the water permeability is always greater than the CO2 permeability through the contactor and indicates that the contactor operating under these conditions will loss water into the CO2 product stream and concentrate the solvent. The water permeability for PTMSP is similar to literature [31] and indicates that competitive sorption of CO2 and MEA on water is not significant within the PTMSP membrane contactor. In contrast, the water permeability through PIM-1 and Teflon AF1600 is lower than literature [31,32]. This may be due to MEA and CO2 competitive sorption on water permeability or alternatively pore wetting changing the hydrodynamics of the porous support.

texts.text: Fig. 6. Permeability (barrer) of H2O through the three membrane contactors as a function of temperature ( ° C).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There was no evidence of MEA in the condensed water for all three membrane contactors, measured by HPLC. This indicates that MEA permeation through the non-porous layer of the three membrane contactors is limited. Hence, the non-porous layer provides the benefit of preventing MEA loss, which is an issue for porous membrane contactors undergoing solvent regeneration as well as in traditional stripping columns.
texts.text: There was no evidence of MEA in the condensed water for all three membrane contactors, measured by HPLC. This indicates that MEA permeation through the non-porous layer of the three membrane contactors is limited. Hence, the non-porous layer provides the benefit of preventing MEA loss, which is an issue for porous membrane contactors undergoing solvent regeneration as well as in traditional stripping columns.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Three thin-film composite membrane contactors, based on nonporous layers of PTMSP, PIM-1 and Teflon AF1600 on porous PP, were investigated for the stripping of CO2 from MEA, for solvent regeneration at temperatures 70 ° C and greater. It was determined that the CO2 regeneration flux for all three membrane contactors increased with temperature, as well as with solvent Reynolds numbers. The overall mass transfer coefficients for the CO2 flux through the non-porous contactors were determined, indicating that the process is mass transfer limited and that the solvent boundary layer is responsible for >90% of the mass transfer resistance. The water fluxes through all three non-porous contactors were orders of magnitude greater than the corresponding CO2 flux.
texts.text: Three thin-film composite membrane contactors, based on nonporous layers of PTMSP, PIM-1 and Teflon AF1600 on porous PP, were investigated for the stripping of CO2 from MEA, for solvent regeneration at temperatures 70 ° C and greater. It was determined that the CO2 regeneration flux for all three membrane contactors increased with temperature, as well as with solvent Reynolds numbers. The overall mass transfer coefficients for the CO2 flux through the non-porous contactors were determined, indicating that the process is mass transfer limited and that the solvent boundary layer is responsible for >90% of the mass transfer resistance. The water fluxes through all three non-porous contactors were orders of magnitude greater than the corresponding CO2 flux.
